content:
5 min read
Just now

--

For six months, I was finishing most of my daily work before lunch.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Nobody noticed.
Not my manager.
Not my teammates.
Not even the people who depended on my reports every morning.
The dashboards kept updating.
The spreadsheets kept arriving.
The emails kept going out.
The tickets kept moving.
Everything looked exactly the same.
The only difference was that Python was doing most of it.
And that realization changed the way I think about software forever.
Not because I became lazy.
Not because I wanted to work less.
Because I finally understood a lesson that separates good developers from exceptional ones:
The biggest productivity gains rarely come from writing more code.
They come from eliminating work entirely.
If you are spending hours every week repeating the same clicks, copying the same data, updating the same spreadsheets, or generating the same reports, you are not doing valuable work.
